caveats: a: The BLS industry is broader than both NAICS 485310 and the narrowed lens.
a: Largest-occupation counts omit a complete wage matrix and are heavily influenced by large platform employers.
a: Driving exposure is kept low because deployable autonomous service is treated mainly in o, not double-counted as ordinary five-year implementation.
rho: No source directly measures five-year implementation across LMM taxi fleets.
rho: Large platforms have software capabilities and data volumes that small operators may not replicate.
rho: Regulated service, accessibility, and driver-pay rules constrain workflow redesign.
e: No source directly identifies eligible firms within frozen n.
e: BLS reports that 92% of taxi-driver jobs are self-employed, illustrating a large population outside the transferable-firm lens but not measuring the firm count.
e: The frozen n is itself a margin-bridged estimate.
s5: No direct control-transfer series exists for the narrowed lens.
s5: Medallion, permit, vehicle, or customer-account sales are not qualifying unless control of transferable operations changes.
s5: Competitive distress can produce liquidation rather than a going-concern transfer.
q: Revenue models vary materially across metered taxi, driver-affiliation, app-dispatched, and contracted accounts.
q: New York regulation is evidence of the mechanism, not a national estimate.
q: Savings may be absorbed by driver retention, insurance, fleet electrification, or accessibility mandates.
d5: Employment projections are not direct quantity forecasts and contain conflicting industry and occupation signals.
d5: Large metropolitan ride-hailing growth may not represent smaller local markets.
d5: Trips depend on tourism, commuting, public transit quality, household vehicle ownership, fares, and regulation.
o: Waymo volume is company-reported and concentrated in selected geographies.
o: Autonomous fleets still require an accountable fleet-management organization, but not necessarily one of the lens's local taxi/dispatch kind.
o: Regulatory reversals, safety events, capital constraints, weather, and accessibility requirements could materially slow displacement.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeated metered, zone, app-dispatched, account, or contracted passenger trips paid by riders, institutions, insurers, or public programs; operators may own vehicles, lease them to drivers, employ drivers, or charge dispatch and affiliation fees.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 485310 mixes global software marketplaces, individual independent drivers, and locally accountable fleet/dispatch businesses whose labor models, economics, and transferability are not coherent in one screen. The lens retains the LMM fleet/dispatch operator population and excludes software-only marketplaces and individual owner-drivers for business-model coherence.
lens.excluded_activities: Global software-only ride-hailing marketplaces, individual owner-drivers, limousine-only and shuttle-only businesses, nonemergency medical transportation classified elsewhere, captive fleets, vehicle lessors without operating responsibility, and financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Locally or regionally controlled taxi and ridesharing fleet or dispatch operators in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that remain accountable for recurring passenger transportation, including contracted and retail trips.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation employment across the broader Taxi and Limousine Service industry, including large ride-hailing platforms and limousine businesses, rather than the narrowed LMM fleet/dispatch lens, and it reports jobs rather than wage-weighted task exposure. I map dispatch, customer service, office, management, software, and analytical work to high or moderate AI exposure; driver and mechanic work receives low exposure except for administrative and diagnostic tasks. Platform-heavy BLS data likely overstates the lens's software and data-science share, while unreported smaller office occupations may offset part of that bias.
caveats: The BLS industry is broader than both NAICS 485310 and the narrowed lens.
Largest-occupation counts omit a complete wage matrix and are heavily influenced by large platform employers.
Driving exposure is kept low because deployable autonomous service is treated mainly in o, not double-counted as ordinary five-year implementation.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from several LMM taxi fleets separating drivers from contractors and platform staff, with measured handle time, dispatch interventions, account billing, claims, onboarding, compliance, vehicle downtime, and AI-pilot savings.
a.rationale: BLS's largest May 2024 industry occupations include 24,300 shuttle drivers/chauffeurs, 16,660 taxi drivers, 6,720 software developers, 4,550 dispatchers, 2,160 general/operations managers, 1,740 office clerks, 1,150 customer service representatives, 1,020 computer-systems managers, 990 data scientists, and 940 automotive mechanics. AI can substitute or avoid hiring in booking, dispatch, trip matching, customer support, driver onboarding, fraud review, account billing, compliance, marketing, and analytics, but ordinary driving and physical maintenance remain the largest resistant task blocks for local operators.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment, not constant-price trip demand: driver occupations include ride-hailing contractors and services outside the narrowed firm lens, while the broader 485300 industry employment projection excludes much self-employment and anticipates productivity and business-model shifts. I use the 9% ten-year driver-occupation growth as positive demand evidence, temper it with the broader industry's 4.6% wage-and-salary decline and convert to a modest five-year quantity range. Independent-contractor growth could overstate demand accessible to fleet operators, while automation could let trips rise despite falling employment.
caveats: Employment projections are not direct quantity forecasts and contain conflicting industry and occupation signals.
Large metropolitan ride-hailing growth may not represent smaller local markets.
Trips depend on tourism, commuting, public transit quality, household vehicle ownership, fares, and regulation.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of trip counts, passenger miles, real fares, wait times, active vehicles, and service quality from large-city taxi/TNC regulators and institutional accounts, segmented between local fleet operators, independent ride-hail drivers, and autonomous fleets.
d5.rationale: BLS expects combined taxi-driver, shuttle-driver, and chauffeur employment to grow 9% from 2024 to 2034, citing continued ride-hailing reliance, older adults' transportation needs, disability and paratransit demand, events, tourism, and business travel. Yet it projects wage-and-salary employment in taxi and limousine service down 4.6%, consistent with ongoing shift toward platforms, contractors, and automation. Underlying paid-trip quantity should grow modestly, but the traditional operator share is addressed separately in o.

caveats: No source directly identifies eligible firms within frozen n.
BLS reports that 92% of taxi-driver jobs are self-employed, illustrating a large population outside the transferable-firm lens but not measuring the firm count.
The frozen n is itself a margin-bridged estimate.
e.change_evidence: Firm-by-firm reconciliation of the 22 frozen-band businesses using state and municipal taxi/TNC licenses, corporate registries, fleet and driver counts, revenue by taxi versus excluded service, customer contracts, normalized EBITDA, and change-of-control rules.
e.rationale: The frozen count covers firms in the financial band but the narrowed lens removes software-only marketplaces, limousine and shuttle businesses, individual owner-drivers, captive fleets, and entities without transferable operating responsibility. Remaining regional fleets and dispatch affiliations have repeat external customers and can qualify, but licenses, medallions, driver relationships, customer accounts, and regulated operating authority must transfer with control.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 485310 ($9,350,653,958) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 485310 ($6,544,615,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $303,483 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 485310); class <5: 2,399 firms, avg revenue/firm $229,474 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,557 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 323 firms, avg revenue/firm $903,548 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $88,819 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 181 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,371,331 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $134,802 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 118 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,968,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $390,054 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,424,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,417,951 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $333,462,929 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,779,406 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 22

o.bridge: Waymo's company-reported autonomous volume and CPUC authorization measure driverless service in selected cities, not the national share of taxi/ridesharing quantity that will cease to require a local LMM fleet/dispatch operator. I use commercial scale as evidence that displacement is real, then discount heavily for geographic limits, regulation, capital intensity, weather, accessibility, remote assistance, fleet service, and slow expansion outside dense markets. The company source may overstate expansion success, while network effects could make displacement faster in covered cities.
caveats: Waymo volume is company-reported and concentrated in selected geographies.
Autonomous fleets still require an accountable fleet-management organization, but not necessarily one of the lens's local taxi/dispatch kind.
Regulatory reversals, safety events, capital constraints, weather, and accessibility requirements could materially slow displacement.
o.change_evidence: City-by-city autonomous fleet counts, paid trips, operating-domain coverage, airport and accessibility capability, regulatory permits, unit economics, remote-assistance staffing, and the resulting closure or share loss of local taxi fleets; also contract awards that explicitly replace human-driven operators.
o.rationale: A large share of five-year trips will still require a local accountable fleet/dispatch operator, particularly institutional, accessible, airport, suburban, and smaller-market service. However, software marketplaces already let independent drivers self-serve dispatch, and autonomous platforms now eliminate the traditional driver and local dispatch layer for material urban volume. Waymo reported 15 million rides in 2025 and over half a million fully autonomous trips per week across ten U.S. cities by March 2026; CPUC authorization shows fare-charging driverless service is legally operational. This creates unusually tangible substitution risk for the narrowed operator type.

caveats: Revenue models vary materially across metered taxi, driver-affiliation, app-dispatched, and contracted accounts.
New York regulation is evidence of the mechanism, not a national estimate.
Savings may be absorbed by driver retention, insurance, fleet electrification, or accessibility mandates.
q.change_evidence: Operator-level contribution margins and fare schedules before and after automation, plus municipal rate-setting files, driver-pay rules, institutional contract pricing and renewal clauses, dispatch-fee changes, and competitor price responses.
q.rationale: Metered and regulated fares can let an operator retain cost savings between rate reviews, while institutional fixed-price trips and dispatch fees can similarly shelter gains during a term. Retention erodes through ride-hailing price competition, driver compensation demands, regulated minimum pay, contract rebids, customer expectations, and reinvestment needed for availability, safety, and accessibility. The five-year mix supports moderate rather than complete capture.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year implementation across LMM taxi fleets.
Large platforms have software capabilities and data volumes that small operators may not replicate.
Regulated service, accessibility, and driver-pay rules constrain workflow redesign.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor hours, service levels, abandoned-call rates, dispatch interventions, deadhead miles, billing denials, fraud losses, and maintenance downtime from AI deployments at comparable taxi fleets, including integration cost and adoption time.
rho.rationale: Local operators can implement proven software and AI functions in dispatch, call handling, trip assignment, estimated arrival, fraud detection, document processing, driver communications, account billing, and vehicle maintenance triage within five years. Fragmented legacy dispatch systems, municipal rules, accessibility, union or driver relationships, cybersecurity, round-the-clock exception handling, and thin management teams keep implementation partial. Autonomous driving is excluded from this estimate and reflected in operator-required demand.

caveats: No direct control-transfer series exists for the narrowed lens.
Medallion, permit, vehicle, or customer-account sales are not qualifying unless control of transferable operations changes.
Competitive distress can produce liquidation rather than a going-concern transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year transaction and ownership histories from municipal license transfers, state registries, PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, bankruptcy dockets, and interviews with owners of every eligible firm regarding age, succession, debt, and willingness to sell.
s5.rationale: A regional operator can transfer fleet, dispatch operations, licenses, accounts, driver network, and institutional contracts, and platform and autonomous competition may motivate succession or consolidation. Conversely, distressed closure, medallion or license transfer without operating-company control, and individual driver turnover do not qualify. Regulatory approvals, fleet liabilities, insurance, and customer concentration reduce executable transfers.

principal_driver: Very high reported compensation intensity and a broad, already-digital dispatch, service, billing, compliance, and analytics workload.
principal_weakness: Commercial autonomous ride-hailing and software marketplaces can displace the local fleet/dispatch operator itself, not merely automate its staff.

caveats: The a proxy starts from a broad, platform-heavy occupation mix rather than payroll-weighted tasks at local fleet and dispatch operators; the disclosed bridge is plausible, but the 0.31 base remains weakly anchored.

S4 supports regulatory and operating mechanisms in New York City, not the national numeric bases for rho, e, s5, q, or d5; those primitives are appropriately labeled ESTIMATE or otherwise bridged, but comparable-operator evidence is absent.

The d5 proxy combines occupation growth driven partly by independent ride-hailing and service categories outside the narrowed lens with declining wage-and-salary industry employment, so its modest positive trip-demand base is directional rather than directly observed.

The o proxy extrapolates company-reported autonomous scale in selected cities to a national five-year operator-required quantity share; geographic rollout, accessibility, capital intensity, and continued fleet-management labor make the 0.68 base highly uncertain despite the wide disclosed range.

The frozen n of 22 covers the broad industry while eligibility and executable transfer shares are unverified for the narrowed lens; because F is 2.12, the CONDITIONAL base tier is especially sensitive to the already disclosed e and s5 estimates.

sources_audited.note: The May 2024 OEWS table confirms the cited Taxi and Limousine Service occupation counts, including 24,300 shuttle drivers and chauffeurs, 16,660 taxi drivers, 6,720 software developers, 4,550 dispatchers, 1,150 customer service representatives, and 990 data scientists. The source population is broader and more platform-heavy than the lens, as the proxy bridge discloses.

The BLS page confirms that 92% of taxi-driver jobs are self-employed, describes driver relationships with dispatch companies and passenger-referral fees, and projects 2024-2034 employment growth of 9% for the combined occupations and 11% for taxi drivers.
The BLS industry table reports Taxi and limousine service (485300) employment of 71.2 thousand in 2024 and 67.9 thousand in 2034, a 4.6% decline.

The report confirms NYC regulation of taxis and for-hire vehicles, permanent Flex Fare rules, increased minimum per-trip rideshare pay, accessibility requirements, and expanded data reporting. It supports the cited mechanisms but is NYC-specific and does not directly measure national LMM values for rho, e, s5, q, or d5.

Waymo's blog supports the company-reported scale claims: 15 million rides in 2025 and more than half a million fully autonomous trips per week across 10 U.S. cities by March 2026. It also describes operations partners and fleet-management workforces.

CPUC Resolution TL-19144 authorizes Waymo to provide passenger service without a safety driver throughout San Francisco and to collect fares, directly supporting legally deployed driverless taxi service.

title: Taxi and Ridesharing Services
